key_insight: TDD loop present — Inspector spec verifies each route returns correct status. But test feedback loop delay measured at 3+ seconds on full suite, above L1 ceiling (1s). The test should fail faster than the user notices the delay. Green test suite is necessary but not sufficient: adversarial specs (malformed inputs, rate limits, injection) not yet present.

56 specs chartered = 56 test sessions properly defined. Red-Green-Refactor visible in spec evolution: Round 1 had 18 failures (Red), Round 2 fixed root causes (Green), specs now stable (Refactor). Missing: adversarial cycle (what happens when malformed input reaches the API?). Inspector is currently a happy-path test suite wearing a QA hat. GLOW 98 target correctly identified: add OWASP-class adversarial specs.

1349 tests passing with zero failures = strong Red-Green discipline. But LEK-F2 confirmed: happy path is still a lie. Codex agents, even with explicit Fallback Ban in CLAUDE.md, write bare except 8 times. The issue is that Fallback Ban is a test-design constraint, not a code-generation constraint. Fix: add specific test cases that FAIL on bare except (assert that specific exception types propagate). That would turn the Fallback Ban into a testable invariant — which is what TDD requires.

Convention-over-configuration applied at the test layer: cpu-nodes/*.md is discovered by convention, NOT config. This is TDD philosophy applied to system architecture. The acceptance test (pip install in /tmp) is the ideal test form: it tests the whole system from the outside, in production-like conditions, with no mocking. 100% CPU hit rate means the test is also the fastest path — convention enabled zero-LLM performance. This is what a well-tested architecture looks like: the tests describe user intention, not implementation details.
